title: Specificity of Political and Socio-Economic Modernization of Soviet Power in Kazakhstan (1917–1953)
overview: Lecture 7 covers the Soviet 'modernization' of Kazakhstan from the establishment of Soviet power and the Civil War (1918–1920) through War Communism, NEP, forced industrialization, the catastrophic collectivization-famine of 1931–1933 (Asharshylyq), the 1937–1938 Stalinist Terror, the deportation of whole peoples to Kazakhstan, and the Kazakh contribution to victory in the Great Patriotic War (1941–1945). The manual defines Soviet modernization as state-led, terror-driven and discontinuous – a 'kazarmennyi (barracks) socialism' that broke the centuries-old nomadic civilization.

coreConcepts.definition: Transition from a traditional/agrarian society to a modern/industrial one. Soviet modernization (from 1918) is a 'national model' driven by state coercion and political terror rather than gradual reform.
coreConcepts.example: American sociologist Samuel P. Huntington in 'Political Order in Changing Societies' showed that modernizing societies are often unstable and violent.

coreConcepts.definition: Bolshevik economic policy during the Civil War (1918–1920): nationalization, abolition of private property and commodity-money relations, universal labor service, surplus appropriation ('prodrazvyorstka') of grain.
coreConcepts.example: Triggered famine: livestock fell to 20% of pre-war levels in places; one third of the republic's population affected.

coreConcepts.definition: Adopted at the X Congress of the RCP(b) in 1921: tax in kind replaced surplus appropriation, land lease and hired labor allowed, small businesses leased to private operators, credit cooperatives and land/water reforms.
coreConcepts.example: By 1925 sown area reached 3 million hectares; livestock roughly doubled compared with 1922. Heavy industry began to be restored. Wound down by Bolsheviks from 1925 in favor of Lenin's 'building socialism' plan.

coreConcepts.definition: Three pillars: (1) Industrialization, (2) Collectivization, (3) Cultural Revolution.
coreConcepts.example: Each was implemented in Kazakhstan from the late 1920s with massive social consequences.

coreConcepts.definition: Creation of large-scale heavy industry. Decided at the 14th Party Congress (1925). In Kazakhstan: raw-materials orientation, forced pace, administrative-directive style, discrimination (use of forced labor of the repressed).
coreConcepts.example: Turksib railway (Alma-Ata to Semipalatinsk, completed 1930 – two years ahead of schedule); restored Ridder and Karsakpay copper smelters and Karaganda mines; built Chimkent lead plant, Balkhash and Dzhezkazgan copper smelters, Ust-Kamenogorsk lead-zinc plant.

coreConcepts.definition: Forced merger of peasant farms into collective farms (kolkhozes), with parallel forced settling of nomads. Course set at the 15th Party Congress (December 1927).
coreConcepts.example: Confiscation of bais decreed 27 August 1928 (1,034 households evicted). Forced sedentarization: 87,136 households in 1930; 242,208 in 1933.

coreConcepts.definition: The Great Famine in Kazakhstan – consequence of forced collectivization and confiscations. On the eve there were 40.5 million head of cattle; by 1 January 1933 only ~4.5 million remained.
coreConcepts.example: Estimates: ~4 million Kazakhstanis died (up to 70% of the dead were Kazakhs); 75% of nomadic and semi-nomadic households fled abroad to China, Iran, Afghanistan. Many scholars (Burkitbay Ayagan, Ziyabek Kabuldinov, Kurt Jonasson) characterize it as genocide.

coreConcepts.definition: National policy 1920–1926: recognition of Kazakh as a state language alongside Russian, translation of office work into Kazakh, formation of national technical intelligentsia, involvement of indigenous people in state work, formation of a national working class.
coreConcepts.example: Curtailed in 1926; many participants repressed. Russian language reintroduced wholesale, but a Kazakh technical intelligentsia and working class had been created.

coreConcepts.definition: Bolshevik ideological program: eliminate illiteracy, form a proletarian culture, propagate socialism through arts.
coreConcepts.example: Literacy reached 76.3% by 1939; 1.1 million people in schools by 1940; 20 institutes and 118 specialized secondary schools.

coreConcepts.definition: 'Barracks socialism' – term used by Kazakh historians for the Stalinist model: complete destruction of the old, denial of cultural and historical continuity, monopoly of one party, terror as the main means of modernization.
coreConcepts.example: Marx-Engels-Lenin-Stalin cult; Communist Party as sole authority; criticism of party decisions banned.

expandedNotes.heading: Establishment of Soviet Power and Civil War (1917–1920)
expandedNotes.body: Soviet power was established between October 1917 and March 1918, slowed by socio-economic backwardness, weak Bolshevik organizations and small working class. Opponents: Cossacks, Socialist-Revolutionaries, Mensheviks, Alash. Establishment was uneven: peacefully in Perovsk (now Kyzylorda – the earliest Soviet centre), most Syrdarya, Akmola regions and the Bukey Horde; by force of arms in Turgay, Ural, Semipalatinsk, Semirechensk. The 1918–1920 Civil War saw the Red Army defend the Bolshevik government against various Russian and interventionist anti-Bolshevik armies. Reds (Bolsheviks) vs Whites (anti-Bolsheviks). Both terrors raged: 'Red Terror' against the 'exploiting class' (landowners, capitalists, defined by social origin); 'White Terror' against revolutionary supporters.

expandedNotes.heading: Soviet Political Structure
expandedNotes.body: Highest legislative body: Soviet of Workers', Soldiers' and Peasants' Deputies. Executive: Soviet of People's Commissars (the government), composed of People's Commissariats by sector (foreign affairs, agriculture, etc.). However, real power lay with the Communist Party – the only legal party. Party Congresses made political AND economic decisions (e.g. the Five-Year Plans). One-party monopoly hindered correct decisions; communists voted unanimously, criticism was banned, leader cults developed. Marxist-Leninist ideology dominated; Marx-Engels-Lenin and later Stalin were the cult figures.

expandedNotes.heading: Evolution of Soviet Statehood in Kazakhstan
expandedNotes.body: FOUR STAGES: (1) Turkestan ASSR (TASSR) – proclaimed at the 5th Congress of Soviets of Turkestan; existed 30 Apr 1918 – 27 Oct 1924; autonomous within RSFSR; population over 5 million; capital Tashkent; territory: Transcaspian, Samarkand, Semirechensk, Fergana, Syrdarya regions. (2) Kyrgyz (Kazakh) ASSR (KASSR) – decree 'On the formation of the Autonomous Kyrgyz Soviet Socialist Republic' signed by Lenin and Kalinin on 26 August 1920; existed until 15 June 1925; population 5,230,000; capital Orenburg, from 1925 Kyzylorda, from 1929 Alma-Ata; territory: Semipalatinsk, Akmola, Turgay, Ural regions, Mangistau district, Caspian region, Bukey Horde. CEC chairman S.M. Mendeshev; SNK chairman V.A. Radus-Zenkovich. (3) The 5th Congress of Soviets of Kazakhstan (April 1925) restored the historically correct name – the republic was now called the Kazakh ASSR; existed 15 Jun 1925 – 5 Dec 1936. (4) Kazakh SSR – formed 5 December 1936 under the new USSR Constitution; existed until December 1991. Population grew from 6 to 16 million; capital Alma-Ata; 19 regions: Aktyubinsk, Alma-Ata, East Kazakhstan, Guryev, Dzhambul, Dzhezkazgan, Karaganda, Kyzyl-Orda, Kokchetav, Kustanai, Mangyshlak, Pavlodar, Semipalatinsk, North Kazakhstan, Taldy-Kurgan, Ural, Turgay, Tselinograd (until 1960 Akmola), Chimkent. Supreme Soviet and its Presidium were the highest organs; the Soviet of Ministers was the government.

expandedNotes.heading: War Communism and Famine (1918–1921)
expandedNotes.body: Industry and transport were devastated by Civil War. War Communism content: nationalization of private property; abolition of commodity-money relations; nationalization of small, medium and large industry; universal labor service; creation of a labor army; introduction of surplus appropriation ('prodrazvyorstka') which removed all surplus grain – and often necessary supplies. Result: a massive famine, starting with jutes (mass livestock death) in which the number of livestock dropped to 20% in some places; one third of the republic's population suffered; Kazakh refugees streamed into the republics of Central Asia and Siberia.

expandedNotes.heading: NEP (1921–1925)
expandedNotes.body: At the X Congress of the RCP(b) in 1921 the Bolsheviks adopted NEP. Content: (1) food tax in kind (less than surplus appropriation, fixed); (2) land lease and hired labor permitted; (3) credit cooperation introduced; (4) small businesses leased to private individuals and cooperatives; (5) land and water reforms returned original lands to Kazakhs that had been given by the tsarist government to the Siberian and Ural Cossack troops. RESULTS: 1925 sown area – 3 million hectares; livestock nearly doubled vs. 1922; steps taken to restore heavy industry. NEP launched commodity-money and market relations – which conflicted with communist ideology, so from 1925 the Bolsheviks began phasing it out.

expandedNotes.heading: Industrialization (1925–1941)
expandedNotes.body: 14th Party Congress (1925) decided to industrialize – goal: strengthen defense, overcome capitalist backwardness, transform an agrarian country into an industrial power. FOUR FEATURES in Kazakhstan: (1) Raw-materials character – extractive industries dominated: coal, oil, ferrous and non-ferrous metals. (2) Forced pace – Turksib railway (Alma-Ata to Semipalatinsk) completed by 1930, two years ahead of schedule. (3) Administrative-directive style – decisions taken without discussion. Prominent Kazakh leaders Sadvakasov and Khodzhanov spoke against raw-material orientation and were accused of 'national deviationism' and repressed. (4) Discrimination – heavy use of forced labor of the repressed. PROJECTS: Ridder and Karsakpay copper smelters; Karaganda coal mines restored; Chimkent lead plant; Balkhash and Dzhezkazgan copper smelters; Ust-Kamenogorsk lead-zinc plant. Industrialization continued until World War II.

expandedNotes.heading: Collectivization and Asharshylyq
expandedNotes.body:
15th Party Congress (December 1927) proclaimed the course toward collectivization. EXCESSES in Kazakhstan – three particular forms:
1) Forced drives into collective farms; intimidation of peasants; socialization of livestock that peasants did not want to part with.
2) Imported workers (from Moscow, St Petersburg etc.) ran collectivization in Kazakh auls without knowing local peculiarities.
3) 'Maly Oktyabr' ('Small October') policy of F.I. Goloshchekin – first secretary of the Communist Party of Kazakhstan – aimed at 'sovietizing the aul' through class struggle. Goloshchekin claimed there were 'many bais in Kazakhstan still exploiting peasants.' Confiscation decree of 27 August 1928 'On the confiscation of bais' economy' – 1,034 households were confiscated and evicted.
Forced sedentarization: 87,136 households moved in 1930, 242,208 in 1933 – farms were dragged together into stationary villages.
DEMOGRAPHY OF DISASTER: livestock fell from 40.5 million on the eve to ~4.5 million on 1 January 1933 (about 90% lost). Mass famine and forced migration. Estimates by leading historians: Prof. Burkitbay Ayagan – 'scale of tragedy so monstrous that we can with full moral responsibility designate it as a manifestation of the policy of genocide'; Prof. Ziyabek Kabuldinov – ~4 million citizens of Kazakhstan died (up to 70% of the dead were Kazakhs); American historian Sarah Cameron ('The Hungry Steppe') – 'one of the worst crimes of the Stalinist regime'; Kurt Jonasson – 'a clear case of genocide'. 75% of available nomadic and semi-nomadic households migrated outside Kazakhstan (to China, Iran, Afghanistan) by the late 1930s. The 1933 letter of T. Ryskulov (Deputy Chairman of the RSFSR Council of People's Commissars) to Stalin documented mass death from famine: corpses removed daily in Auliye-Ata, Chimkent, Semipalatinsk, Kyzyl-Orda; the hungry ate garbage, roots of wild plants, small rodents; cases of corpse-eating; overcrowded orphanages; high child mortality.

expandedNotes.heading: Cultural Revolution
expandedNotes.body: Goal: form a proletarian culture propagating socialism. The image of the worker-communist became honorable; other art was prohibited. ACHIEVEMENTS: literacy reached 76.3% by 1939; 1.1 million people in schools by 1940; 20 institutes and 118 specialized secondary schools. People's Commissariat of Education of the Kazakh ASSR formed October 1920 with A. Baitursynov as Commissar. 1922–1923: 14 Kazakh-language textbooks published ('Physics', 'Grammar', 'Pedagogy', 'Algebra', 'Geography'). In the 1920s–1930s the state invested heavily – 367 schools built; 361 renovated. MUSIC: A. Kashaubayev appeared in Paris (1925) to French acclaim. A.V. Zatayevich's '1000 Songs of the Kirghiz (Kazakh) People' (1925) and '500 Kazakh Songs and Kuyis' (1931) gained international fame. 13 January 1934: Kazakh State Musical Theater opened on the base of the Drama Theater's music studio. End of 1938: Alma-Ata newsreel studio organized; first sound documentary 'Dzhambul ata' produced.

expandedNotes.heading: Stalinist Terror
expandedNotes.body: Repressed: Alash leaders (Bukeikhanov, Baitursynov, Dulatov, Zhumabaev) – mostly executed 1937–1938. Kazakh literary lions Saken Seyfullin, Ilias Zhansugurov, Beimbet Mailin – executed. Statesmen Turar Ryskulov, Sadvakasov, Khodzhanov – executed. ALZHIR (Akmola Camp for Wives of Traitors of the Motherland) operated 1938–1953. KarLAG, Steplag, Dzhezkazganlag and other camps formed the GULAG archipelago across Kazakhstan; more than 1 million people passed through KarLAG. Mass DEPORTATIONS: Koreans from the Far East (1937, ~100,000); Germans from the Volga (1941); Chechens, Ingush, Crimean Tatars, Balkars, Karachais, Meskhetian Turks (1944). Total ~1.5 million people of 30+ nationalities deported to Kazakhstan.

expandedNotes.heading: Contribution to the Defeat of Nazi Germany
expandedNotes.body: From 22 June 1941, 1.2 million Kazakhstanis were called up from a republic with a population just over 6 million; ~200,000 more were already in the Red Army – about 20% of the population. The remaining ~80% forged victory in factories and farms; women and children bore most of the burden. More than 600,000 Kazakhstanis died on the fronts. 12 rifle and 4 cavalry divisions, 7 brigades and 50 separate regiments were formed in the republic. Over 100,000 received military orders and medals; more than 500 received the title Hero of the Soviet Union – including the Kazakh girls Manshuk Mametova (machine-gunner) and Aliya Moldagulova (sniper). Four Kazakh pilots – Talgat Bigeldinov, Leonid Beda, Ivan Pavlov, Sergei Lugansky – received the title twice. Thousands of Kazakhs were active in the partisan movement in occupied territories. On 30 April 1945, Kazakh Lieutenant Rakhimzhan Koshkarbayev was the first to attach the red flag of Victory to the Reichstag. ECONOMY: 220+ factories evacuated; 1.5 million evacuees; lead 85%, molybdenum 60%, copper 35% of all Soviet production came from Kazakhstan; '9 of 10 bullets were cast from Kazakh lead' (Tokayev); 1,500 wagons of clothing and food sent to the front. Kazakhstan became the main livestock-breeding base in the east. 700,000+ Kazakhstanis drafted into the labor army at defense enterprises.

keyScholars.name: Vladimir Lenin
keyScholars.contribution: Leader of the Bolsheviks; signed the 26 August 1920 decree creating the Kyrgyz (Kazakh) ASSR.

keyScholars.name: Joseph Stalin
keyScholars.contribution: Soviet leader; architect of forced industrialization, collectivization and the Great Terror.

keyScholars.name: F.I. Goloshchekin
keyScholars.contribution: First Secretary of the Communist Party of Kazakhstan (1925–1933); author of the 'Maly Oktyabr' policy. Repressed 1941.

keyScholars.name: S.M. Mendeshev
keyScholars.contribution: First Chairman of the CEC of the Kazakh ASSR (from 1920). Repressed.

keyScholars.name: V.A. Radus-Zenkovich
keyScholars.contribution: Chairman of the SNK of the Kazakh ASSR from 1920.

keyScholars.name: Turar Ryskulov
keyScholars.contribution: Deputy Chairman of RSFSR Council of People's Commissars; sent a 1933 letter to Stalin documenting mass starvation in Kazakhstan. Repressed.

keyScholars.name: Smagul Sadvakasov
keyScholars.contribution: Kazakh statesman; opposed raw-materials industrialization; accused of 'national deviationism' and repressed.

keyScholars.name: A. Khodzhanov
keyScholars.contribution: Kazakh statesman; opposed raw-materials orientation; repressed.

keyScholars.name: Saken Seyfullin
keyScholars.contribution: Founder of Kazakh Soviet literature; first chairman of the SNK after 1922; repressed.

keyScholars.name: A. Baitursynov
keyScholars.contribution: Linguist and first People's Commissar of Education of the Kazakh ASSR (October 1920); repressed.

keyScholars.name: Bauyrzhan Momyshuly
keyScholars.contribution: Officer of the 8th Guards (Panfilov) Rifle Division; defender of Moscow.

keyScholars.name: Aliya Moldagulova
keyScholars.contribution: Sniper; Hero of the Soviet Union (posthumous).

keyScholars.name: Manshuk Mametova
keyScholars.contribution: Machine-gunner; first Asian woman awarded Hero of the Soviet Union (posthumous).

keyScholars.name: Talgat Bigeldinov
keyScholars.contribution: Pilot; twice Hero of the Soviet Union.

keyScholars.name: Sarah Cameron
keyScholars.contribution: American historian; 'The Hungry Steppe: Famine, Violence, and the Creation of Soviet Kazakhstan' (2018) – key Western study of the famine.

keyScholars.name: Kurt Jonasson
keyScholars.contribution: Western scholar who called the Kazakh famine a 'clear case of genocide'.

keyScholars.name: Robert Kindler
keyScholars.contribution: German scholar; 'Stalin's Nomads: Power and Famine in Kazakhstan' (2018).

comparativeTables.title: Soviet Statehood in Kazakhstan: Four Stages
Soviet Economic Policies
rows: Nationalization, surplus appropriation, labor army → famine
Tax in kind, land lease, private trade → recovery
Heavy industry, raw-materials orientation, forced pace

comparativeTables.title: Deportations of Whole Peoples to Kazakhstan

realWorldExamples: ALZHIR Memorial-Museum near Akmola/Astana preserves the site of the Akmola Camp for Wives of Traitors of the Motherland.
The KarLAG Museum in Dolinka, Karaganda region, documents life in the largest GULAG complex in Kazakhstan.
Panfilov Park in Almaty commemorates the 8th Guards (Panfilov) Rifle Division and its defenders of Moscow.
The Eternal Flame and Glory Memorial in Almaty honor 600,000 Kazakhstanis who died in WWII.
31 May is marked annually in Kazakhstan as the Day of Remembrance of Victims of Political Repressions and Famine.
President Tokayev's 2024 statement that '9 of 10 bullets were cast from Kazakh lead' is widely quoted in commemorations of the war effort.

timeline.event: October Revolution; Bolsheviks come to power
Soviet power established across most of Kazakhstan
Turkestan ASSR proclaimed in Tashkent
Civil War; Red and White Terror
Kyrgyz (Kazakh) ASSR formed; capital Orenburg; A. Baitursynov People's Commissar of Education
X Congress RCP(b) adopts NEP
Republic renamed Kazakh ASSR; capital moved to Kyzylorda; Goloshchekin becomes 1st Secretary; 14th Party Congress decides to industrialize
Decree 'On confiscation of bais' economy'
Capital moved to Alma-Ata; collectivization accelerates
Turksib railway completed
Asharshylyq – Great Famine; ~4 million dead, 75% of nomads flee abroad
T. Ryskulov letter to Stalin documenting famine
Kazakh State Musical Theater opened in Alma-Ata
Kazakh SSR formed under new USSR Constitution
Deportation of Koreans; mass executions of Alash and literary leaders
Great Terror – Bukeikhanov, Baitursynov, Ryskulov, Seyfullin executed
ALZHIR camp opens; Alma-Ata film studio organized
Germans deported to Kazakhstan; Great Patriotic War begins
Great Patriotic War; 1.2 million Kazakhstanis at the front, 600,000+ killed
Deportation of Chechens, Ingush, Crimean Tatars, Balkars, Karachais, Meskhetian Turks
Rakhimzhan Koshkarbayev plants red flag on the Reichstag
Death of Stalin – ALZHIR closes

mustMemorize: Asharshylyq 1931–1933: livestock 40.5M → 4.5M; ~4 million deaths; 75% of nomads emigrate
Industrialization: raw-materials orientation, forced pace, administrative-directive style, discrimination
Korenizatsiya 1920–1926: Kazakh as state language; ended 1926
ALZHIR = Akmolinskii Lager Zhen Izmennikov Rodiny (1938–1953)
WWII: 1.2 million fighters; 600,000+ dead; 500+ Heroes of Soviet Union; 9 of 10 Soviet bullets from Kazakh lead
Kazakh Heroes: Bauyrzhan Momyshuly, Aliya Moldagulova, Manshuk Mametova, Talgat Bigeldinov, Rakhimzhan Koshkarbayev
cheatSheet:
1918 TASSR → 1920 KASSR (Orenburg) → 1925 Goloshchekin → 1928 confiscation → 1930 Turksib → 1931–33 famine → 1936 Kazakh SSR → 1937–38 Terror → 1941–45 WWII
Lenin's 3 pillars: Industrialization + Collectivization + Cultural Revolution
Famine = Stalinist genocide per most Kazakh historians
mnemonics: ALZHIR = A-kmola L-ager Zh-yon I-zmennikov R-odiny
40.5 → 4.5: lose a decimal point in 4 years (collectivization)
B-M-M-B-K: Bauyrzhan, Manshuk, Moldagulova, Bigeldinov, Koshkarbayev – Kazakh war heroes
